title: Berlin after 1945
strengths: Official city history with explicit postwar population snapshot and original city population benchmark.
limitations: Snapshot is after the battle and war's end, not a battle-average exposure series.
notes: Key wording: 'only 2.8 million of the city's original population of 4.3 million still live in the city.'

citation_short: Museum Berlin-Karlshorst, 'The End of the War in Berlin'
title: English - The German Surrender in May 1945
pages_or_sections: Section 'The End of the War in Berlin', lines 54-57 in web capture
strengths: Clear city-battle framing with separate military and civilian deaths and explicit victim-side wording ('Red Army' versus 'German side').
limitations: Short museum summary; methodology for the rounded numbers is not explained on-page.
notes: This is the strongest city-tight direct-death source for Berlin in the accessible set.

title: Berlin: The Downfall 1945 (OCR PDF proxy)
pages_or_sections: P162 (population and Volkssturm), P242 (defender strength versus 1.5m Soviet troops)
strengths: Widely used scholarly narrative with explicit quantitative statements on early-April population and city defenders.
limitations: Narrative source; some figures are approximate and some military numbers refer to the Berlin Defence Area under extreme fluidity.
notes: Accessible OCR lines show 3-3.5 million civilians in early April and ~45,000 Wehrmacht/SS + just over 40,000 Volkssturm defending against 1.5 million Soviet troops.

title: Seelow Heights and Berlin
author_or_org: David Porter / Military History Matters / The Past
strengths: Provides a city-assault troop figure (464,000) tighter than the full strategic offensive totals.
limitations: Magazine-style secondary synthesis rather than a primary document; should be used as a bounding anchor, not sole denominator source.
notes: Key wording: 'The Soviet forces assembling for the attack on the city comprised five armies and four tank armies – 464,000 men ... On 26 April, they completed the encirclement of Berlin.'

title: The Battle of Berlin: Germany's downfall on the Eastern Front
pages_or_sections: Main article; lines 87-91 in web capture and associated search snippet
strengths: Reputable museum synthesis and explicit acknowledgement of uncertainty around civilian losses.
limitations: 125,000-civilian figure is not clearly limited to the municipal urban phase and is framed as hard to calculate.
notes: Useful as a high-end contextual ceiling for civilian deaths, not as the sole city-core estimate.

citation_short: National WWII Museum, 'The Battle for Vienna'
title: The Battle for Vienna
pages_or_sections: Opening section, lines 159-162 in web capture
strengths: Good city-battle chronology and clear description of defenders as a depleted urban garrison.
limitations: No explicit troop counts or city-specific death totals on the page.
notes: Useful for dating the urban phase and identifying that the city was defended mainly by the depleted II SS Panzer Corps plus ad hoc units.

title: Exhumierungen im Nachkriegs-Wien
author_or_org: Wien Geschichte Wiki / City of Vienna (search snippet only)
pages_or_sections: Search snippet; page fetch failed in session
date_scope: 1945-04-06/1945-04-13 (start date partly truncated in accessible snippet)
strengths: Local municipal-history source likely tied to burial/exhumation reconstruction; directly distinguishes German military and civilian dead.
limitations: Only search-snippet text was accessible in-session, so methodology and full date wording could not be inspected directly.
notes: Snippet reports roughly 19,000 German military dead and roughly 2,300 civilians dead.

citation_short: Vienna Press Service historical archive, April 1945
title: April 1945 - Historische RK 1945
pages_or_sections: Search snippet for 13 April; additional snippet for 6 April
strengths: Explicitly dates city combat and preserves a contemporaneous broadcast claim separating German and Soviet military dead.
limitations: The 19,000/18,000 figures are attributed to Radio Moscow, so they are contemporaneous but potentially propagandistic and should not be treated as uncritically verified.
notes: Also supports use of 6 April as the clear start of fighting on Vienna city territory.

title: Wien seit 1945: die Metamorphose einer Stadt (web mirror)
author_or_org: Andreas Weigl et al. / web mirror
strengths: Provides multiple displacement/outflow anchors rather than a single static postwar population count.
limitations: Access was through an unofficial web mirror; battle-week population still requires scenario construction.
notes: Reports 110,000 evacuees by late September 1944, -170,000 net migration Jan 1944-Mar 1945, another 200,000 leaving when fighting approached, and a July 1945 low point of 1,323,758.

title: 1945 bis heute
author_or_org: Wien Geschichte Wiki / City of Vienna (search snippet only)
pages_or_sections: Search snippet; page fetch failed in session
strengths: Official municipal-history snippet giving a clean pre-battle baseline for Groß-Wien.
limitations: Only the search snippet was accessible in-session, not the full page or underlying citation chain.
notes: Snippet wording: 'Hatte man 1939 im damaligen Groß-Wien noch 1,93 Millionen an "ständiger" Wohnbevölkerung gezählt ...'

citation_short: Modern War Institute, Aachen case study
title: Urban Warfare Project Case Study #10: Battle of Aachen
author_or_org: Liam Collins, Jayson Geroux, John Spencer / Modern War Institute
source_type: military analysis / urban warfare case study
pages_or_sections: Opening and 'The Battle' sections, lines 39-56 in web capture
strengths: Clear urban-battle framing with useful remaining-civilian and defender-strength ranges.
limitations: Secondary synthesis; does not provide a clean city-specific civilian direct-death series.
notes: Reports 165,000 pre-battle residents, 5,000-20,000 remaining at the start, ~18,000 German personnel in sector, and 4,600-6,000 defenders in Aachen itself.

title: Vienna offensive
author_or_org: Search snippet summarizing an encyclopedic page with cited literature
pages_or_sections: Search snippet and excerpted siege section
strengths: Convenient operation-wide ceilings and identifies which Soviet armies were involved in the city assault.
limitations: Operation-wide, not city-specific; encyclopedic summary rather than a direct reading of the cited German and Soviet volumes. Used only as a bounding prior.
notes: Useful to ensure city-assault estimates stay far below operation-wide totals such as 1,171,800 Soviet and 270,000 Axis personnel.
